name: Alabama

quarter.design: Helen Keller seated reading a book in braille, with her name in English and braille and a longleaf pine branch and magnolias around her.

funFacts: Alabama was the birthplace of the U.S. Space and Rocket Center, where the Saturn V moon rocket was developed in Huntsville.
The state has more navigable inland waterways than any other state in the U.S.
Montgomery was the first capital of the Confederacy before Richmond, Virginia took over.

name: Alaska

quarter.design: A grizzly bear emerging from the water with a salmon in its mouth, with the North Star above.

funFacts: Alaska is the largest state in the U.S., more than twice the size of Texas.
Denali (Mount McKinley) is the tallest mountain in North America at 20,310 feet.
Alaska was purchased from Russia in 1867 for about 2 cents per acre.

name: Arizona

quarter.design: The Grand Canyon with a saguaro cactus in the foreground, separated by a banner that reads 'Grand Canyon State'.

funFacts: The Grand Canyon is up to a mile deep, 18 miles wide, and 277 miles long.
The saguaro cactus, which can grow over 40 feet tall, lives only in the Sonoran Desert.
Arizona has the largest contiguous Ponderosa pine forest in the United States.

name: Arkansas

quarter.design: A diamond, rice stalks, and a flying mallard duck above a lake, representing the state's mining, agriculture, and waterfowl.

funFacts: Arkansas is home to the only active diamond mine in the United States, at Crater of Diamonds State Park, where visitors can keep what they find.
Hot Springs National Park has 47 natural thermal springs flowing at 143 degrees Fahrenheit.
The state has more than 600,000 acres of lakes and 9,700 miles of streams and rivers.

name: California

quarter.design: John Muir admiring Yosemite Valley's Half Dome, with a California condor soaring overhead.

funFacts: California has the tallest tree (a redwood named Hyperion), the largest tree by volume (General Sherman, a giant sequoia), and the oldest tree (a bristlecone pine over 4,800 years old).
Death Valley is the hottest, driest, and lowest place in North America at 282 feet below sea level.
If California were a country, its economy would be one of the largest in the world.

name: Colorado

parks.name: Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park

quarter.design: A sweeping view of the Rocky Mountains with evergreen trees and a banner reading 'Colorful Colorado'.

funFacts: Colorado has the highest average elevation of any state, with more than 50 mountains over 14,000 feet tall.
Denver is called the Mile-High City because it sits exactly 5,280 feet above sea level.
The world's largest flat-top mountain, Grand Mesa, is in Colorado.

name: Connecticut

quarter.design: The Charter Oak, a famous tree where Connecticut's royal charter was hidden in 1687 to protect it from being taken away by the king.

funFacts: The first hamburger and the first lollipop are both said to have been made in Connecticut.
Connecticut is home to the oldest U.S. newspaper still being published, the Hartford Courant, founded in 1764.
The Mark Twain House in Hartford is where the author wrote some of his most famous books, including The Adventures of Tom Sawyer.

name: Delaware

quarter.design: Caesar Rodney on horseback, racing 80 miles to Philadelphia to cast the deciding vote for American independence on July 2, 1776.

funFacts: Delaware was the first state to ratify the U.S. Constitution on December 7, 1787.
It is the second-smallest state in the U.S., only larger than Rhode Island.
Delaware has no sales tax, making it a popular place for shopping trips.

name: District of Columbia
funFacts: Washington, D.C. was designed by French-born architect Pierre Charles L'Enfant in 1791.
The District is home to the White House, the U.S. Capitol, and the Supreme Court — the three branches of the U.S. government.
No buildings in D.C. can be taller than the Capitol dome, which is why the city has no skyscrapers.

name: Florida

quarter.design: A 16th-century Spanish galleon, a space shuttle, and a strip of land with sabal palm trees, with the words 'Gateway to Discovery'.

funFacts: Florida has the longest coastline in the contiguous 48 states, with about 1,350 miles.
St. Augustine, founded in 1565, is the oldest continuously inhabited European-founded city in the U.S.
The Everglades is the only place in the world where alligators and crocodiles live side by side.

name: Georgia

quarter.design: A peach, the state's nickname symbol, framed by sprigs of live oak and a banner reading 'Wisdom, Justice, Moderation'.

funFacts: Georgia is the largest state east of the Mississippi River by land area.
Coca-Cola was invented in Atlanta, Georgia in 1886 by pharmacist John Pemberton.
The Okefenokee Swamp is one of the largest freshwater wetlands in North America.

name: Hawaii
motto: Ua mau ke ea o ka aina i ka pono

quarter.design: King Kamehameha I stretching his hand toward the Hawaiian Islands, with the motto 'Ua Mau Ke Ea O Ka Aina I Ka Pono'.

funFacts: Hawaii is the only U.S. state made up entirely of islands — 137 in total, though only 7 are inhabited.
Mauna Kea, measured from its base on the ocean floor, is the tallest mountain in the world at over 33,000 feet.
Hawaii is the only state that grows coffee commercially.

name: Idaho

parks.name: Craters of the Moon National Monument

quarter.design: A peregrine falcon with the outline of Idaho behind it and a banner reading 'Esto Perpetua' meaning 'Let it be perpetual'.

funFacts: Idaho grows about one-third of all the potatoes eaten in the United States.
Hells Canyon, on the Idaho-Oregon border, is the deepest river gorge in North America — deeper than the Grand Canyon.
Nearly every type of gemstone has been found in Idaho, including the rare star garnet, found in only one other place in the world.

name: Illinois

quarter.design: A young Abraham Lincoln in front of the state outline, the Chicago skyline, and a farm scene with 21 stars (Illinois was the 21st state).

funFacts: Abraham Lincoln spent most of his life in Illinois, which is why the state is also called 'The Land of Lincoln'.
The first skyscraper in the world was built in Chicago in 1885 — the 10-story Home Insurance Building.
Illinois has more than 1,000 miles of bike trails and is home to the famous Route 66 highway.

name: Indiana

quarter.design: A racecar overlaid on the state outline with 19 stars (Indiana was the 19th state) and a banner reading 'Crossroads of America'.

funFacts: The Indianapolis 500, one of the most famous car races in the world, has been held in Indiana every year since 1911.
Wabash, Indiana was the first city in the world to be lit entirely by electric streetlights, in 1880.
Indiana is one of the leading producers of popcorn in the U.S.

name: Iowa
motto: Our liberties we prize and our rights we will maintain

quarter.design: A one-room schoolhouse with a teacher and students planting a tree, inspired by an artwork by Grant Wood, with the words 'Foundation in Education'.

funFacts: Iowa is the only state whose east and west borders are both formed entirely by rivers — the Mississippi and the Missouri.
Iowa produces more corn than any other state, growing about 2.5 billion bushels each year.
The famous painting 'American Gothic' was painted by Iowan Grant Wood in 1930.

name: Kansas

quarter.design: An American bison standing in front of a field of sunflowers, two of the state's most famous symbols.

funFacts: Kansas is one of the leading wheat-producing states and is often called the 'Breadbasket of America'.
The geographic center of the lower 48 states is in Lebanon, Kansas.
Dorothy and Toto from 'The Wizard of Oz' are from Kansas, and the state really does see many tornadoes each year.

name: Kentucky

parks.name: Land Between the Lakes National Recreation Area

motto: United we stand, divided we fall

quarter.design: A thoroughbred racehorse behind a fence with a stately mansion in the background, with the words 'My Old Kentucky Home'.

funFacts: Mammoth Cave in Kentucky is the longest known cave system in the world, with more than 420 miles explored.
The Kentucky Derby horse race has been run at Churchill Downs in Louisville every year since 1875.
Fort Knox in Kentucky stores most of the United States' gold reserves.

name: Louisiana

parks.name: Cane River Creole National Historical Park

quarter.design: A pelican, a trumpet with musical notes, and the outline of the Louisiana Purchase territory, with the words 'Louisiana Purchase'.

funFacts: Louisiana is the only U.S. state that uses parishes instead of counties.
New Orleans is the birthplace of jazz music, which began in the early 1900s.
The Louisiana Purchase in 1803 doubled the size of the United States by buying land from France for about 3 cents an acre.

name: Maine

parks.name: Katahdin Woods and Waters National Monument

quarter.design: The Pemaquid Point Lighthouse on a rocky coast with a schooner sailing in the background.

funFacts: Maine produces about 90 percent of the lobster caught in the United States.
Maine has more coastline than California, with about 3,500 miles when you count all the bays and islands.
About 89 percent of Maine is covered in forest — more than any other state.

name: Maryland

quarter.design: The dome of the Maryland Statehouse with white oak leaf clusters, and a banner reading 'The Old Line State'.

funFacts: The Chesapeake Bay in Maryland is the largest estuary in the United States.
Francis Scott Key wrote 'The Star-Spangled Banner', America's national anthem, in Baltimore Harbor during the War of 1812.
Maryland is sometimes called 'America in Miniature' because it has mountains, beaches, farmland, and forests in a small area.

name: Massachusetts

parks.name: Boston Harbor Islands National Recreation Area

motto: Ense petit placidam sub libertate quietem

quarter.design: An outline of the state with a Minute Man statue, honoring the 1775 Revolutionary War soldiers, and the words 'The Bay State'.

funFacts: The first Thanksgiving was held in Plymouth, Massachusetts in 1621.
Boston has the oldest subway system in the United States, opened in 1897.
Harvard University, founded in 1636, is the oldest college in the United States.

name: Michigan

quarter.design: An outline of the state showing both peninsulas, with all five Great Lakes labeled around it.

funFacts: Michigan touches four of the five Great Lakes and has more freshwater coastline than any other state.
Michigan is shaped like a mitten, and locals often point to a spot on their hand to show where they live.
Detroit is known as the 'Motor City' because Henry Ford built the first mass-produced cars there.

name: Minnesota

parks.name: Mississippi National River and Recreation Area

quarter.design: Two people fishing on a lake with a loon floating in the foreground, plus a forest and the state's outline, with the words 'Land of 10,000 Lakes'.

funFacts: Minnesota actually has more than 11,000 lakes, not just 10,000 as the nickname suggests.
The Mississippi River starts in Minnesota at Lake Itasca, where you can walk across it on stepping stones.
The first Scotch tape, Post-it Notes, and water skis were all invented in Minnesota.

name: Mississippi

quarter.design: Two magnolia blossoms with the words 'The Magnolia State' on a banner.

funFacts: The Mississippi River, which forms the state's western border, is the second-longest river in the U.S.
Root beer was invented in Biloxi, Mississippi in 1898.
The teddy bear got its name from President Theodore Roosevelt after a famous hunting trip in Mississippi in 1902.

name: Missouri

quarter.design: Lewis and Clark and Sacagawea's son Pomp returning down the Missouri River, with the Gateway Arch in the background and the words 'Corps of Discovery'.

funFacts: The Gateway Arch in St. Louis is the tallest monument in the United States at 630 feet.
Missouri is where Lewis and Clark started their famous expedition west in 1804.
The ice cream cone was invented at the 1904 World's Fair in St. Louis.

name: Montana

quarter.design: A bison skull above a landscape of rivers, mountains, and forests, representing Montana's wilderness and Native American heritage.

funFacts: Montana is sometimes called 'Big Sky Country' because of its huge open spaces and sweeping views.
Glacier National Park has more than 130 named lakes and 700 miles of hiking trails.
More dinosaur fossils have been found in Montana than in any other state.

name: Nebraska

quarter.design: Chimney Rock, a famous landmark on the Oregon Trail, with a covered wagon and pioneers traveling beside it under the sun.

funFacts: Nebraska is the only state in the U.S. with a one-house (unicameral) legislature.
The Sandhills cover one-fourth of Nebraska and are one of the largest sand dune regions in the Western Hemisphere.
Arbor Day was started in Nebraska City in 1872 — a holiday all about planting trees.

name: Nevada

quarter.design: Three wild mustangs galloping across the desert, with the sun rising behind mountains and sagebrush in the foreground.

funFacts: Nevada is the driest state in the U.S., with most of it covered by desert.
About 86 percent of the land in Nevada is owned by the federal government — more than any other state.
The Hoover Dam, on the Nevada-Arizona border, is one of the largest concrete structures in the world.

name: New Hampshire

quarter.design: The 'Old Man of the Mountain', a famous rock formation that looked like a man's face, with nine stars and the state motto.

funFacts: Mount Washington in New Hampshire holds the record for one of the highest wind speeds ever recorded on land — 231 mph.
New Hampshire was the first colony to declare independence from England, six months before the Declaration of Independence.
The Old Man of the Mountain, a famous rock face that gave the state its symbol, fell down in 2003.

name: New Jersey

parks.name: Delaware Water Gap National Recreation Area

quarter.design: George Washington and his soldiers crossing the Delaware River on Christmas night in 1776, based on the famous painting.

funFacts: New Jersey is the most densely populated state in the U.S. — lots of people in a small space.
Thomas Edison did most of his most famous inventing in New Jersey, including the light bulb and the phonograph.
The first organized baseball game was played in Hoboken, New Jersey in 1846.

name: New Mexico

quarter.design: The state outline with a Zia sun symbol, used by the indigenous Zia Pueblo people, and the words 'Land of Enchantment'.

funFacts: Santa Fe, New Mexico is the oldest state capital in the U.S., founded by the Spanish in 1610.
Carlsbad Caverns has a chamber so big it could fit six football fields inside it.
Albuquerque hosts the world's largest hot-air balloon festival every October.

name: New York

quarter.design: The Statue of Liberty in front of an outline of the state with the Hudson River, and the words 'Gateway to Freedom'.

funFacts: New York City was the first capital of the United States after the Constitution was ratified.
Niagara Falls, on the New York-Canada border, is one of the most powerful waterfalls in the world by water flow.
The Adirondack Park in upstate New York is bigger than Yellowstone, Yosemite, and the Grand Canyon combined.

name: North Carolina

quarter.design: The Wright Brothers' first airplane in flight at Kitty Hawk in 1903, with the words 'First Flight'.

funFacts: The Wright Brothers made the first successful airplane flight at Kitty Hawk, North Carolina in 1903.
Mount Mitchell in North Carolina is the highest peak east of the Mississippi River at 6,684 feet.
North Carolina was the first state in the U.S. to open a public university, the University of North Carolina, in 1795.

name: North Dakota

parks.name: Knife River Indian Villages National Historic Site
Fort Union Trading Post National Historic Site

motto: Liberty and union, now and forever, one and inseparable

quarter.design: Two grazing bison with sunset over rugged Badlands buttes in the background, showing the state's wide-open prairie.

funFacts: The geographic center of North America is in North Dakota, near the town of Rugby.
North Dakota leads the country in growing sunflowers, flaxseed, and honey.
The International Peace Garden on the border with Canada celebrates the long friendship between the two nations.

name: Ohio

parks.name: Perry's Victory and International Peace Memorial

motto: With God, all things are possible

quarter.design: An early airplane and an astronaut, honoring Ohio's role as the 'Birthplace of Aviation Pioneers' — home of the Wright Brothers and astronauts John Glenn and Neil Armstrong.

funFacts: Ohio is the birthplace of seven U.S. Presidents — more than any state except Virginia.
Neil Armstrong, the first person to walk on the moon, was born in Wapakoneta, Ohio.
The Rock and Roll Hall of Fame is in Cleveland, Ohio, the city where the phrase 'rock and roll' was first popularized.

name: Oklahoma

quarter.design: A scissor-tailed flycatcher in flight over a field of Indian blanket wildflowers.

funFacts: Oklahoma is home to 39 federally recognized Native American tribes — more than any other state.
The world's first parking meter was installed in Oklahoma City in 1935.
Oklahoma has the largest deposit of pink granite in the world, found in the Wichita Mountains.

name: Oregon

parks.name: John Day Fossil Beds National Monument

quarter.design: Crater Lake, the deepest lake in the U.S., with Wizard Island and surrounding evergreen trees.

funFacts: Crater Lake in Oregon is the deepest lake in the United States at 1,949 feet — formed inside a collapsed volcano.
Oregon has the only U.S. state flag with two different designs on its front and back.
The shortest river in the world, the D River, is in Oregon and is only 120 feet long.

name: Pennsylvania

quarter.design: A statue called 'Commonwealth', an outline of the state, and a keystone, with the words 'Virtue, Liberty, Independence'.

funFacts: The Declaration of Independence and the U.S. Constitution were both signed in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania.
Hershey, Pennsylvania, is the chocolate capital of the U.S. — even the streetlamps are shaped like Hershey's Kisses.
Pennsylvania is home to the oldest continuously operating drive-in movie theater in the country, opened in 1934.

name: Rhode Island

parks.name: Blackstone River Valley National Historical Park

quarter.design: A vintage sailboat racing across Narragansett Bay, with the Pell Bridge in the background and the words 'The Ocean State'.

funFacts: Rhode Island is the smallest U.S. state — only 48 miles long and 37 miles wide.
Despite the name, Rhode Island is not an island — but the state does include more than 30 islands.
Rhode Island was the first of the 13 colonies to declare independence from Britain, on May 4, 1776.

name: South Carolina

quarter.design: The state outline with a Carolina wren, a sabal palmetto tree, and a yellow jessamine flower — all state symbols.

funFacts: Fort Sumter in Charleston Harbor is where the first shots of the Civil War were fired in 1861.
South Carolina grows more peaches than Georgia, even though Georgia is called the Peach State.
The only commercial tea plantation in the United States is on Wadmalaw Island, South Carolina.

name: South Dakota

quarter.design: Mount Rushmore featuring the four presidents' faces, a Chinese ring-necked pheasant in the foreground, and wheat ears framing the design.

funFacts: Mount Rushmore, with the carved faces of four U.S. Presidents, took 14 years to finish and stands 60 feet tall.
The Badlands of South Dakota contain one of the richest fossil beds in the world.
South Dakota has more shoreline miles of lakes than the entire state of Florida.

name: Tennessee

parks.name: Big South Fork National River and Recreation Area

quarter.design: Three musical instruments — a fiddle, a trumpet, and a guitar — and a banner reading 'Musical Heritage', honoring Tennessee's three musical regions.

funFacts: Tennessee borders eight other states, tied with Missouri for the most state neighbors.
Nashville is called 'Music City' and is the home of country music and the Grand Ole Opry.
Great Smoky Mountains National Park, partly in Tennessee, is the most visited national park in the U.S.

name: Texas

quarter.design: An outline of the state with a single bright star to the side, and a lariat encircling the design with the words 'The Lone Star State'.

funFacts: Texas is the second-largest U.S. state, big enough that El Paso is closer to California than to Houston.
Texas was its own independent country, the Republic of Texas, from 1836 to 1845 before joining the U.S.
Texas has more cattle and more farms than any other U.S. state.

name: Utah

quarter.design: An old steam locomotive and a modern train meeting at Promontory Point, marking the completion of the transcontinental railroad in 1869.

funFacts: Utah is the only state where every county contains some part of a national forest.
The Great Salt Lake is the largest salt lake in the Western Hemisphere.
Utah has five national parks: Arches, Bryce Canyon, Canyonlands, Capitol Reef, and Zion.

name: Vermont

quarter.design: A man tapping a maple tree to collect sap, with Camel's Hump mountain in the background and the words 'Freedom and Unity'.

funFacts: Vermont produces more maple syrup than any other state — about 2 million gallons a year.
Vermont was an independent republic for 14 years before joining the U.S. in 1791.
About 78 percent of Vermont is covered in forest — one of the most forested states in the country.

name: Virginia

quarter.design: The three ships of Jamestown — the Susan Constant, Godspeed, and Discovery — that brought the first English colonists in 1607, with the words 'Jamestown 1607-2007 Quadricentennial'.

funFacts: Eight U.S. Presidents were born in Virginia — more than any other state.
Jamestown, founded in 1607, was the first permanent English settlement in North America.
The Pentagon, the headquarters of the U.S. Department of Defense, is in Arlington, Virginia and is one of the world's largest office buildings.

name: Washington

parks.name: Mount St. Helens National Volcanic Monument

quarter.design: A king salmon leaping in front of Mount Rainier, with the words 'The Evergreen State'.

funFacts: Washington is the only state named after a U.S. President — George Washington.
Mount Rainier is the most glaciated peak in the contiguous U.S., with 25 named glaciers.
Washington grows more apples than any other state — about two-thirds of all the apples eaten in the U.S.

name: West Virginia

quarter.design: The New River Gorge Bridge, one of the longest single-arch bridges in the world, spanning a deep mountain canyon.

funFacts: West Virginia is the only state created by separating from another state — it split from Virginia during the Civil War in 1863.
The state's average elevation is 1,500 feet — the highest of any state east of the Mississippi River.
The New River in West Virginia is one of the oldest rivers in the world, despite its name.

name: Wisconsin

quarter.design: A cow, a round of cheese, and an ear of corn, with the words 'Forward', representing Wisconsin's dairy and farming traditions.

funFacts: Wisconsin produces more cheese than any other state — about 3.4 billion pounds a year.
Wisconsin has 15,074 lakes — more than 6 lakes per square mile in some areas.
The ice cream sundae was invented in Two Rivers, Wisconsin in 1881.

name: Wyoming

quarter.design: A bucking horse with a rider, the same image used on Wyoming license plates, with the words 'The Equality State'.

funFacts: Wyoming is the least populated U.S. state — fewer people live there than in many single cities.
Yellowstone, mostly in Wyoming, was the world's first national park, established in 1872.
Wyoming was the first state in the U.S. to give women the right to vote, in 1869.
